speaker: interviewer
text: --[INAUDIBLE] if you could join the theater. What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never forget. I know even-- even the name, the real name of him, Otto [INAUDIBLE]. And I went. He was in piano. He asked me to sing something in German language. I didn't know any German songs. I said, I'm very sorry. I don't know. Oh, you [? do ?] [? not ?] know. But Polish song? Yes, I know. And I try to think remember from a Polish film. Because we had before the war also Polish films, comedy films. And it was beautiful songs about our [? Lwow, ?] Lemberg city. And I thank him. This something very good, very nice way. He tried even to play this and I sing together with this piano. It sounded wonderful. He said, OK. Take a seat. Wait for a minute. Probably he discussed with somebody. Because after that, he left, he came smiling. And he said to me, OK. You are employed. You'll be there. You will belong to our chorus singers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I was so happy. I went to my mother. I will work. But my mother said, be carefully because-- no. This thought my brother-- Be carefully because in the arts there are many queers people. I didn't hear this word never before. I was very young. For me, homosexuals and queer, I never heard before. I said, what you are thinking queer? Yes. Maybe later you will see. Be wary. Be wary, he said to me. But I didn't-- I wasn't worried. I was happy because I have seen nice people. I have seen dancers, musicals.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started with a gypsy [INAUDIBLE], this melody I never forget. And then [INAUDIBLE] [? the ?] [? young, ?] the son of the [GERMAN] [? with ?] [? his ?] from [INAUDIBLE]-- from [INAUDIBLE]. And the melody from Johann Strauss, wonderful. I have also one unique picture of this gypsy. When I was in the war troupe, I was sitting and somebody-- later, I knew that this gay boy, Sigmund Kay, also. He made this picture of-- of me. And so I was-- I had to-- I have to join this job morning by baker and evening in the other. But with my nice job by baker, once they was finished-- because the beautiful lady, the woman of the baker-- she liked me not only as the delivery boy of bread, but even of her lovers' letters. She was 20 years younger than the husband, and I understood later that she expected something more, not from me because I was still a child. But she had many lovers in secretly. And she used me as a letter boys. And it's happened that something once they [? rung, ?] I was fallen. There was not the lover, but it was the wife of her lover-- of lover. And when I said her that once [? these ?] happened something wrong, probably she wanted that I will leave because she was afraid-- she afraid that it can be blackmailed or like this. And rather for her marriage, it was very good. The-- the husband for her was very good. What she wanted, what she liked, she had everything because she was young. She was very pretty. And he did everything for her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, I had to leave my job in bakery against-- against wish of the baker. He was wondering why before his wife appreciated so much that I will work there, and now she-- oh, better when-- when he leaves our job. It's better maybe. But I [? find that ?] a other job-- not so good like bakery-- in a [? manufactury. ?] It was Krueger-- Hans Krueger mineral oil. It was petrol company work for all kind of oils et cetera. The-- the job was very dirty. Not so nice, but what I have to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to work in theater. So I had to work by this company, by this Hans Krueger. Thanks to this [? boss's ?] [? wife, ?] I wasn't sent to the forced camp in Germany. So I could stay with my mother, with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that in the theater, you meet-- you met another gay boy, Sigmund. How-- how did you know that he was gay?

speaker: interviewee
text: His behavior was so differently. And some of other Polish-- because [? there ?] were-- not me-- not-- I'm not-- I wasn't alone Polish singer. There were some of Polish, even girls too. And his behavior was so differently. So I heard-- especially one older joke at him, called him as a gay in very bad meaning. In Polish language he said, pedal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And pedal, I never heard [? what's ?] there even. I ask my brother what's [? meaned ?] it. Where did you heard this? In the theater. You see? I told you. There are many of pedals-- there are many working of pedals in this theater. But you- did he explain you what it means?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He didn't. No, no. Only that it's very bad. And-- but he didn't explain me that they are the men who loves men. He didn't do that. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand it at that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Slowly, yes. Slowly I [? felt. ?] I did. Because as the children, you know, we play and this. Even my brother once, he-- he-- did something with me. But it was in very simple way. It was so stupid way without saying this is this kind of love. No, it is like children. Probably all children has did in this age like me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other gay people in the theater besides Sigmund?

speaker: interviewee
text: Among chorus singers, no. But probably some tenors because their watching was so completely different. Even I was observed by others. I have seen. You see, I like it work, and I make me what is [INAUDIBLE] say. Before you go on the stage, you take to-- you must take on the face some color and this et cetera. And once one tenor said, oh, you look beautiful. I'd never told me that I was a nice looking boy. And [? then ?] [? this ?] [? started ?] [? problem. ?] And Sigmund, he joined. He's take-- he's keen for you, he told me [INAUDIBLE]. In Polish, it was [POLISH]. It's mean, he's looking for me [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And you you went on working in the German company and in the theater in 1940 and in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was working, exactly it was 1940. And I must to state to you that this [? path-- ?] night path was very good for me, not only for walking. For walking it was not good because some of Polish people-- they were sitting home. They have seen me on the street. They thought maybe I'm collaborating with Germany or maybe I'm a spy or [? some. ?] And it was very wrong for me. I couldn't explain to anybody. I'm working in the theater. I am correct with my Polish nationality. I am still Polish, et cetera. But there were some German chaps. And I could buy without any stamps, nothing. When I went at this time, they thought that I am German. I tried to ask maybe, please give me this-- two or three words I said in German. They couldn't recognize. I bought some marmalade-- like jars of marmalade-- and I took for home because our ration was so limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I never forget one day it was exactly the date on 4th November, 1941. I left the theater. I had still a little time to go home. I passed a little the streets. I wanted to-- to be relaxed, and I-- I bought this jar of jam. And then I have seen somebody watched me. Watched me. And then it was so dark. We had only gas lamp. Who is watching me first? I was very anxious. Maybe some from the Gendarme-- Gendarme, the soldiers, you know. But then I have seen some soldier. He crossed me a little, and he smile at me. Nobody-- no soldier smiled to me. His uniform was gray, very nice, better than the other green. He had on his sides [? a ?] small-- I don't know it's called, not knife. You know, the soldiers they had to-- this was like decoration [INAUDIBLE]. And then he pass. He smile. And he-- I have seen his nice face, et cetera. And he asked me, hello. Hello? Good evening. Good evening. Never, heil Hilter. Usually they greeted with, heil Hitler. Oh, what you are doing? I'm going home. This I said, [GERMAN]. It was not so difficult to say in German. [GERMAN]. It's too early. It's 10 o'clock, early. You have time still. OK. He invited me to the near-- to the cafe. Of course the cafe was-- was not so-- it was instant-- we said in German during the war, [GERMAN]. And here we were there. I tried to speak to less I could because I-- my knowledge of German language was very short and very broke. And then he ask, oh. So you are going home? Where you are living? [INAUDIBLE]? And I-- I told him this very bad place. This was [INAUDIBLE]. Very bad. Probably he knew. And you were born here? He recognize because German people never were living in so bad [INAUDIBLE]. And he said, you are-- are-- you are-- you are born here? Yes, I am born. I am Polish. Polish? What you are doing now? Don't worry. I'm working the theater. I have my pass. I can stay until 12 o'clock. Oh, good. But there were some other people sitting. Probably he was afraid that somebody will hear our talk in this bad German. And he said, come on. [? Shall ?] [? we ?] [? go? ?] So you live there in Torun Mokre. It mean Torun Mokre, very bad [INAUDIBLE]. And then, yes, this way. And he went, may I accompany you? Yes, you can. Because I have seen-- I took special streets, no more people, nothing. I never forget it was [? snowy-- ?] on the first snow in November. And I like it to be nice looking boy in theater. I took half shows, and I was good looking. But for the snow, it was-- the snow was so high and very deep. I never forget. He took me like my father took me when I was a child on his back. And he passed me to the other street over the snow. It was very nice. And-- and then he said, now it's right. It's OK. He treated me as never-- nobody before. And he asked me, so is this you? Yes, I'm in-- It's better you stay here. We cannot go there. I didn't want that somebody will see that I was accompanied by a German soldier. And then he asked me, may we see us again? I said, what for? I would like to talk you, to walk a little, maybe to go to cafe. You have the pass [INAUDIBLE]. There is no matter. There will be very-- Yes, as you like. OK. But when? You see, tomorrow I cannot. After tomorrow, no. But it is Sunday. Are you working still? If I'm not working, I can get out. I have my passport. And we made some appointments for Sunday. And-- but where? At this same place. It was station. It was in Torun [? Central ?] [INAUDIBLE] Station. During those three days, I was thinking, where will go with him? Because to the cafe, I cannot go. What we should do? To theater. There was no-- I couldn't go as a visitors. I had no passport. [INAUDIBLE] And then-- but when I came-- he was waiting before I came. And he ask, where should we go? You know some places maybe? Not here in the theater. I know for you it's very difficult because I'm a soldier. But I am not German soldier. You're not German? Who are you? I'm Austrian. Oh, then my [? stone ?] was falling from my heart because you must know all Polish people do not like Germany. They hate because they suffered long time always. Poland was divided. One part-- even during the first war-- to German, the other part to Russian. And I was [? to ?] educated that the German people, they are not good people. They are our enemies. And I-- I met a soldier, German soldier. But when he said he's Austrian, I was a little peacefully relaxed. And then I said, where we shall we go? [INAUDIBLE] you are Austrian, but your uniform is still German. Yeah. That's true, yeah. And then I think as a child I-- I knew many places in [? Torun ?] beside the center of city. We walk. We talk. He talk to me a little [? of ?] Austria. He wanted to know about my singing in the theater, about my family, brother-- just usually talking and talking. And then I remembered very nice [INAUDIBLE] in the fields with mountains. And I told him, you see, maybe we shall go there. You are sure? Yes, I'm sure. [INAUDIBLE] We can talk because it is-- he understood more danger, maybe more for you than for me. The Polish people are sitting home, but some soldiers they will see you. That will be not good. They will ask what happened with this young boy. And correct. Fine. When we came, I said, oh, it's a nice place. He took his-- it was, as I told you, cold. He had his coat. He took his coat. He hang there. And he took me in his arm. He said, you are very nice boy. You are pretty boy. Am I really? Nobody said me this. Nobody? He looked at me at my eyes. I look at him, and he kissed me. Oh my god. He [INAUDIBLE] [? kissed ?] [? me. ?] And then he said, it was friendship kiss. Only friendship, nothing more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Thanks. I kiss him also because friendship. And we were talking. We are singing. And he asked me then for the [? next. ?] Because I don't [INAUDIBLE] [? details. ?] The first days, you must know, there was nothing done. There was very romantic. I was very romantic as a Polish people. And his behavior was also romantic, very dedicated. He tried to probably to receive my-- my [? honest ?] or my-- my assurance. He wanted to find my-- as a friend. Because he tried to bring me something. Some sweets, some-- once he brought me some one apple, even one orange. Orange in the war, you can understand. They had, but we never. And when I needed later some shoes, some shirt, he brought me also. Even he gave me some [INAUDIBLE]-- for-- this is for military soldiers. And during my [? walk in the ?] evening, I could go to the [INAUDIBLE]-- to the bakery and to buy some-- of course, there is also instant pastry, but it was [? good-- ?] [? So ?] [? that ?] he got a good-- as a friend from me-- [? dual ?] [? duties ?] what he made for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was very glad. Me too. My behavior was changed. Of course my mother feel at home. My brother feel at home. They seen the change. You must understand. It was war. And then I wanted to look more nicer, not to the people in theater, but I wanted to look to see better for him. So I asked my mother to wash my shirts. Every day I change another one. And she asked me, you see? We have no soap. And when we receive one piece of soap for one month, it was so bad quality, you couldn't do nothing with the soap. How she washed the shirts, I don't know. Until now, it is unbelieved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, I was-- and my brother also-- she-- he-- he feels something. And I feeled also that he is doing something important with all of us. We met very often some friends, his friends. And when I came in, they stop to talk. I think they didn't believe me because I am working in the German theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was the next [? state ?] of my life and the middle of April 1942. [? Once ?] [? they-- ?] [? then ?] when we had this place, we never meet-- we never met us in other places-- only in [? there. ?] He was a little older than me. I came, and I have seen his face was very white and so sad. I ask him, Willi, what happened? Why are you so sad? You know, I must leave you. Why? Why you must leave? Why? I must go. Tomorrow I am going to the eastern front to the Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I heard that this Russia front was the worst. Very few of soldiers returned back. But I come back. Because we made so nice plan. He told me the war will be not all time. One day you will see. You'll go to Vienna with me. You will know my family. We will live together. It will be so wonderful. You are so good fellow, so honest, completely like [? older ?] people. I like you so much. I love you so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, you love me? I love you, too. And then just now he said to me, this information-- he gave me this information-- [INAUDIBLE] information. It was shock for me and also for him. But-- and I tried to cry some tears. Don't cry. It makes me more worse. It makes me more heavy. Don't do that. You can be sure. I write you so quickly as possible. You promise me? Yes, I promise you. And so it was our last-- last thing. Then I worked as usual in theater. And I waited. I was waiting for his letter. I never receive his letter. This, as I mentioned in the theater, this Sigmund Kay-- I told him something that I met a wonderful soldier. Really? And then he told me in this bad word, pedal. Don't say. He wasn't this. What do you mean? He was a real, very nice fellow. [? What? ?] Let me [INAUDIBLE]. I-- never I wanted to show somebody this place where I spent so nice days or nights.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once-- I never forget this. It was in August or June or July, maybe. It was rather warm. And we had no performances at this day. But he had also this pass for the night. We went to this shed. [? I ?] [? want-- ?] he wanted to see. He want-- and me, I wanted also to see once more this place where I spent so nice time. It was something-- it happens something because we felt there is somebody. First, I was thinking maybe from the police, from the German police. Somebody came to us and said-- asked in Polish language-- what we are doing. We said we are also Polish. We don't do nothing. I said, you're not-- [INAUDIBLE] No, we are not German. And then I have seen two others, as especially very young-- maybe in my age, 17 years old. Very nice boy. And he said-- and he told me they escaped from the Stuttgart concentration camp. My god. It was very danger because it was always written [GERMAN]. If somebody help for the escapers, to give the clothes et cetera, will pay with his own life. But this Sigmund, as usually, he was so stupid and this. But at this moment, he was completely different change. He said, don't worry. You-- I show you the way in town. Maybe you can escape in this and this way. But the one, he said, but we have dresses from the Stuttgart-- from the-- then what we have. It was summer. I had my underwear shirt and my-- this shirt, I gave to this one and Sigmund gave to this young boy this-- later I knew-- he told me that his-- he could talk a little few words German. Pascal-- he told us that he was a Jew from France-- from France. And Sigmund said-- he was-- he said, I take-- I take you home. I give you more dresses. And he took him. And the two other prisoners, they were still staying. But he show them the way how they could leave the shed without any danger for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next day in the theater, I met Sigmund and I asked what happened about-- don't ask too [? more. ?] It's better if you know less-- better for you. And I wanted to know what happened with Pascal. And he make me [INAUDIBLE]. It's wonderful guy. Oh, Then? I feel that he had something with him. And it was all because later, we-- our performances-- the gypsy was no more over. The next one was [GERMAN]. It was-- he didn't play at this operetta, Sigmund. So my contact with-- was less and less with Sigmund.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I didn't received no news from Sigmund, one day during the [GERMAN] operetta, I have seen a part of newspaper. And there was written-- there was the address that the families were writing, for members of soldiers, for friends to ask [? in ?] [? there. ?] I wrote this-- I address this street secretly. And I came home. I was fighting with my thought what I have to do. Maybe I will write to the-- the letter to Willi. I didn't receive from him. Maybe he couldn't write. Maybe I write to him. It will be a great pleasure, a great joy to him-- for him. And I did it. I never forget this letter. I wrote in my child meaning. It was written, Dear Willi, I miss you very much. I-- I never received a letter from you. I never go to this place where we were always. I-- I am thinking always about you. I cannot sleep. I prayed for the god that you come healthy again and that we stay together for the whole life. Please write me as soon as possible. I'm so anxious for you. I thank you in advance for your letter with many kisses and greetings. Yours, this and this. And this letter I sent [? stupid ?] with my [? sender. ?] It was also big fighting in my soul whether to put or not to put. I put it my [? sender. ?] I never received a reply. But one day, it was exactly--
